pages.content: CADI's Strategic Aims Teach well, consistently well, every session, every time, for every student. We will support and encourage academic colleagues and the wider university community to teach consistently well, in any mode of study, through innovative and authentic learning design, teaching and assessment practices. Become a national and international reference point in advancing innovation and effectiveness in teaching, learning and assessment. We will foster a forward-thinking, needs-driven approach to professional development and maximising the impact of digital technologies in higher education. CADI's Priorities and Future Projects Timeline and dates for projects that the CADI team are working on and will be working on over the next 12 - 24 months. 1. Authentic assessment, assessment choice and embracing AI in assessment design. 2. Awarding gaps. 3. Innovative, collaborative, cost-effective and scalable learning design at module and course levels. 4. A future-focused digital learning agenda, in line with the Digital Success Plan for L&T and Education Strategy (figure 2), to support staff and students in maximising digital technologies for learning, teaching and assessment. 5. Moodle integrity, sustainability, security and functionality. 6. pages.content: Welcome to AI at the University of Portsmouth. Discover how artificial intelligence is transforming higher education. This collection of pages brings together practical guidance, inspiration and critical perspectives to help you explore and engage with AI in meaningful, ethical and pedagogically sound ways. Whether you’re curious about the capabilities of generative tools, looking to redesign assessments, or seeking examples of practice from colleagues, you’ll find resources to support you at every stage. Our aim is to empower staff and students alike to navigate this evolving landscape with confidence, creativity and care. View video in Panopto View transcript AI Guidance for Teaching Staff This guidance sets out the University of Portsmouth’s position on generative AI in learning, teaching and assessment. It encourages staff to educate themselves about and embrace AI rather than attempt to police or ban its use. Core Principles: Educate and embrace AI use – don’t police or ban it AI should enhance, not replace academic practices or roles Focus on learning and authenticity, not detection or suspicion (Co-)design assessments to integrate AI transparently and ethically Our summative assessments should provide credible evidence that each student has achieved learning outcomes, authored their own work, and can demonstrate mastery of the work they submitted. pages.excerpt: Welcome to Connected Curriculum at the University of Portsmouth The Connected Curriculum outlines a new approach to course design that supports inclusive practice and prepares students for a rapidly changing world. pages.content: Welcome to Connected Curriculum at the University of Portsmouth The Connected Curriculum outlines a new approach to course design that supports inclusive practice and prepares students for a rapidly changing world. Replacing the previous Curriculum Framework, it will be adopted from September 2026 and applied across all home courses from September 2027. This guidance has been developed to help course teams begin engaging with the new principles, whether they are designing new courses or reviewing what already exists. Information & resources This page collates the full Connected Curriculum document, the What is Connected Curriculum summary PDF, the 2025 Learning and Teaching Conference keynote video and information about CADI’s Connected Curriculum deep dive workshops. Together, these resources provide context and practical advice to support the use of the Connected Curriculum. A flexible approach Course teams are encouraged to take a flexible approach, making choices that support meaningful learning and a strong sense of progression. The Connected Curriculum does not prescribe a single way of working but offers a structure for creating courses that are relevant, inclusive and academically sound. Support is available from the Centre for Academic Development and Innovation (CADI) for teams who would like help with designing or reviewing their curriculum and assessment approaches. pages.content: Welcome to Enterprise and Entrepreneurship Education Enterprise education is not just about business creation because it encompasses a broader set of skills, mindsets and competencies that are valuable in many aspects of life and work, not only in starting a business. Enterprise education aims to develop an enterprising mindset - creativity, problem-solving, adaptability and initiative. These qualities are important in any context, whether someone is launching a business, working in a company or engaging in social causes. The Student Startup Team is a part of the Careers and Employability Department and empowers students, graduates and university staff through enterprise and entrepreneurship education. The team provides tailored support for students and recent alumni looking to explore, launch, or grow their own business ideas, offering guidance, resources and practical opportunities. In addition to working with students and graduates, the team actively collaborates with academic and professional staff to embed entrepreneurial thinking into the curriculum and co-curricular activities, fostering a culture of innovation across the institution. pages.content: This guidance aims to provide our staff with a deeper understanding of how our students experience life at the University of Portsmouth. In this context, we are not focusing on the teaching, facilities, and qualifications our institution offers, but rather on the complete personal experience of being away from home, gaining independence, entering a new social environment and navigating unfamiliar situations. This guidance is aimed at both academic and professional services staff, as our work at the University affects student experience, whether we have regular contact or not. However, this may be particularly useful to our staff who interact with students regularly, so we can understand their issues and concerns more fully. Student Finances: Living and Learning Under Pressure Rising living costs are reshaping student life in the UK. Surveys and reports reveal greater reliance on part-time work and parental support, growing financial hardship and the impact these pressures have on wellbeing and the student experience. Explore Finances Transitioning to Higher Education Starting university is an exciting but challenging step. From adapting to new academic expectations to managing independence and building a sense of belonging, this stage brings opportunities and pressures. Resources and support can help students navigate the change with confidence. Explore Support Identity, Disadvantage and Belonging A sense of belonging is vital for student wellbeing and achievement. pages.content: A high-value, high-impact collaborative approach designed to help you Teach Well, Consistently Well. Express your interest for Collaborative Growth If you’re interested in enhancing your teaching practice through supportive, peer-led collaboration and taking part in Collaborative Growth then please express your interest by emailing cadi@port.ac.uk Looking for fresh, creative ways to enhance your teaching and learning practice? Collaborative Growth offers an exciting opportunity to connect with a colleague and a learning designer to develop bespoke innovative approaches to your teaching and learning. This collegiate partnership will support you to redesign a specific activity, explore engaging assessment techniques or make the most of digital tools like Moodle or Padlet. This initiative will help you to Teach Well, Consistently Well and enhance your students’ learning experience by doing so. How it works… Identify a specific area of teaching you’d like to enhance. Get paired with a colleague and a learning designer to plan, develop, and trial tailored approaches. Spend up to five hours over the academic year, with flexible scheduling to suit your workload and goals. Pairings are matched based on your areas of focus and unique skills for a mutually beneficial exchange. Build your practice while gathering evidence aligned with the Professional Standards Framework 2023 and PDR discussions. View the Collaborative Growth pdf to find out more. pages.content: Introducing Teach Well - Principles to Practice Teach Well: Principles to Practice is an exciting new Professional Development module that is FREE for UoP staff and is ideal for new starters, early career academics and staff in roles related to teaching or related to supporting student learning. This professional development module is 30 credits at level 7, and will include asynchronous preparation activities and development activities designed to support and provide context. Participants will embark on a pedagogical journey through three pillars of practice for teaching well in HE and will gain confidence to critically evaluate learning and design approaches and reflect on what it means to teach well across different modes of study. On completion of the module, participants will have gained an understanding of UoP's Blended and Connected approach, underpinned by the principles of Active Blended Learning, and understand what it means to Teach Well, Consistently Well. The three pillar days are intentionally designed to support engagement with Advance HE’s Professional Standards Framework (PSF 2023), providing structured opportunities for reflection and build confidence engaging with the PSF. This practical module focuses on learning design, teaching practice, and assessment and feedback, in the context of a solid pedagogic framework. pages.title: UPSU Partnership Agreement | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: This Partnership Agreement outlines the shared purpose, values, principles and practices that underpin the working relationship between the University of Portsmouth (UoP) and University of Portsmouth Students’ Union (UPSU) as the recognised channel for the student voice ensuring impactful collaborat…
pages.content: This Partnership Agreement outlines the shared purpose, values, principles and practices that underpin the working relationship between the University of Portsmouth (UoP) and University of Portsmouth Students’ Union (UPSU) as the recognised channel for the student voice ensuring impactful collaboration, meaningful co-creation and robust independence. It should be read in conjunction with the UoP Student Voice Policy, the UPSU Code of Practice and the shared Financial Memorandum of Understanding. It also acknowledges that the relationship between the University and the SU is governed by appropriate regulation and legislation, including the requirements of the 1994 Education Act, the 2018 Data Protection Act and UK GDPR regulation with regard to data sharing. The Union will use this agreement to help inform annual elected officer and student representative training and working practices, to act as a check and balance when campaigning and acting as agents for change, and to steer the unique relationship with the University upholding the principles of both partnership and independence. pages.title: Current Position on Exams (June 2025) | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Core Message The University’s preferred position is to phase out traditional exams –especially those that simply test memory– in favour of more inclusive and authentic assessments, which are both AI-resilient and AI-embracing, as appropriate. We continue to encourage the use of presentations, practi…
pages.content: Core Message The University’s preferred position is to phase out traditional exams –especially those that simply test memory– in favour of more inclusive and authentic assessments, which are both AI-resilient and AI-embracing, as appropriate. We continue to encourage the use of presentations, practicals or vivas that are appropriately aligned to learning outcomes. In-class tests can also be accommodated. Our aim is to support the design of meaningful assessment strategies that enhance learning, close awarding gaps and reduce over-reliance on exams, while still allowing for their appropriate use in clearly justified cases. Exams can still be used when: Required by PSRBs (Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies) The format authentically reflects professional practice (e.g. simulating real-world scenarios in different disciplines) There is no viable alternative to test specific learning outcomes. Exams should be avoided when: They serve primarily as recall tests (rote memory) They do not align with the intended learning outcomes They contribute to broadening awarding gaps They pose logistical or accessibility barriers for students . To note: This is a University-level position, not a school-by-school preference CADI training, development and advice are aligned with this institutional view Assessments should be designed to reflect inclusive, real-world and student-relevant tasks Definition of “examination” or “exam” A formal, summative assessment conducted under specified c

pages.content: From September, academic skills resources currently hosted on Moodle and Google sites will be migrated to the Library website . Following migration, these legacy sites will no longer be accessible to staff or students. This change does not alter the commitment to academic skills provision. Students will continue to receive high-quality support from tutors, working in closer partnership with Library staff following the migration. The Library website is a well-established platform that students already use to support their learning. Migrating resources to this environment will improve visibility and accessibility, ensuring students can access academic development support in a familiar and trusted space. It will also enable improved usage analytics, allowing us to better understand engagement with resources and ensure future development is informed by student need. On the Library website, resources will sit under the new heading Academic Development , reflecting a broader and more holistic approach to student learning and progression as lifelong learners. Resources will include subject-specific examples where appropriate; however, the emphasis will be on consolidation and consistency to ensure an equitable experience for students across all faculties. Making these resources publicly available will also enhance accessibility for prospective students and support transition into higher education.

pages.content: We would like to share important updates regarding the future of Wiseflow and our strategic direction for digital assessment. Moving beyond Wiseflow Following a central review, we are planning to sunset Wiseflow in summer 2026 and transition our license to an “archive-only” model. While Wiseflow has supported a range of assessment activities, it has not fully met the expectations we originally set. We believe this change will enable us to deliver a more effective, flexible and sustainable assessment experience for our community. Key dates and actions To support a smooth transition, please note the following: All student submissions in Wiseflow must stop before 1 August 2026. Any new assessments with marking dates beyond this point should be set up using Moodle-based tools (e.g. Moodle Assignment, Turnitin, Moodle Quiz). Wiseflow should not be used for the summer Ref/Def period, to avoid any disruption to access for students and staff. Enhanced assessment solutions in Moodle Throughout 2025/26, the CADI team has been developing and refining a range of improved assessment solutions within Moodle. This work has been supported by our move to the cloud with Catalyst, significantly improving system stability, scalability, and enabling rapid development, including AI-assisted enhancements. pages.content: Introducing Teach Well - Principles to Practice Teach Well: Principles to Practice is an exciting new Professional Development module that is FREE for UoP staff and is ideal for new starters, early career academics and staff in roles related to teaching or related to supporting student learning. This professional development module is 30 credits at level 7, and will include asynchronous preparation activities and development activities designed to support and provide context. Participants will embark on a pedagogical journey through three pillars of practice for teaching well in HE and will gain confidence to critically evaluate learning and design approaches and reflect on what it means to teach well across different modes of study. On completion of the module, participants will have gained an understanding of UoP's Blended and Connected approach, underpinned by the principles of Active Blended Learning, and understand what it means to Teach Well, Consistently Well. The three pillar days are intentionally designed to support engagement with Advance HE’s Professional Standards Framework (PSF 2023), providing structured opportunities for reflection and build confidence engaging with the PSF. This practical module focuses on learning design, teaching practice, and assessment and feedback, in the context of a solid pedagogic framework. pages.content: What are cookies? Cookies and similar technologies are very small text documents or pieces of code that often contain a unique identification code. When you visit a website or use a mobile application, a computer asks your computer or mobile device for permission to save this file on your computer or mobile device and gain access to information. Information collected through cookies and similar technologies may include the date and time of the visit and how you use a particular website or mobile application. Why do we use cookies? Cookies ensure that we can see how our website is used and how we can improve it. What type of cookies do we use? Necessary cookies These cookies are necessary for the website to function properly. Performance cookies These cookies are used to gather statistical information about the use of our website, also called analytics cookies. We use this data for performance and website optimization. Functional cookies These cookies enable more functionality for our website visitors. These cookies can be set by our external service providers or our own website. The following functionalities may or may not be activated when you accept this category. - Live chat services - Watch online videos - Social media sharing buttons - Login to our website with social media Advertising / tracking cookies These cookies are set by external advertising partners and are used for profiling and tracking data across multiple websites. We welcome submissions that capture the spirit of this theme through thoughtful pedagogic design, meaningful student engagement, inclusive practice and effective use of learning environments - whether in person or online, synchronous or asynchronous. Contributions can draw on research, professional experience and student partnership to inform and enhance practice. Submissions are welcome across a number of thematic areas including (but not limited to): learning design, student engagement, gamification, assessment and feedback, AI, student support and guidance, equality, diversity and inclusion, digital technologies, education for sustainable development and employability and enterprise. Submissions are welcome from all University staff and our collaborative partners. Submissions co-authored (and ideally co-facilitated) with students will be particularly welcome. pages.excerpt: CADI along with Faculty Heads are making a strong recommendation that all online exams moving forward use Moodle Quiz instead of Wiseflow . This decision follows significant recent improvements to our system infrastructure: Enhanced Capacity and Reliability: Moodle has been migrated to Catalyst IT E…
pages.content: CADI along with Faculty Heads are making a strong recommendation that all online exams moving forward use Moodle Quiz instead of Wiseflow . This decision follows significant recent improvements to our system infrastructure: Enhanced Capacity and Reliability: Moodle has been migrated to Catalyst IT Europe, a leading cloud Moodle provider, ensuring robust performance and scalability. Successful Pilot of Respondus LockDown Browser: This tool replaces the legacy Safe Exam Browser, providing a secure and streamlined exam environment and was successfully piloted in recent TECH in-class tests. New Reporting Features: Moodle will soon include enhanced reporting to help detect potential multi-collusion attempts by students, strengthening academic integrity. These changes position Moodle as the most reliable and secure platform for online assessments. Further guidance materials about Respondus Lockdown browser and multi-collusion reporting will be circulated via the CADI website in the coming weeks. If you have any questions or need support in setting up Moodle quizzes, please contact cadi-digital@port.ac.uk . We hope this news will be received well by academic teams who have had many successful years using the Moodle quiz to support online exams.

pages.content: As some of you may have noticed, at the start of January, Padlet updated our domain from uop.padlet.org to padlet.com/uop . They've reassured us that you can continue using Padlet as usual: all your existing Padlet links and embeds will automatically redirect, and using uop.padlet.org will simply take you to a redirect message for the new domain, where you should log in with your Google account as usual. If you notice anything unexpected, please contact CADI Digital on cadi-digital@port.ac.uk , but we expect everything to run smoothly so you can continue creating, sharing, and posting without interruption.

pages.title: CPD Moodle Upgrade 17th – 21st November | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: We will be upgrading our CPD Moodle site to Moodle 4.5 between 17-21st November. The site will be unavailable to staff and students during this time. Our CPD Moodle site is mainly used to support pre-sessional students and assessments during the summer months whilst our regular Moodle site is being…
pages.content: We will be upgrading our CPD Moodle site to Moodle 4.5 between 17-21st November. The site will be unavailable to staff and students during this time. Our CPD Moodle site is mainly used to support pre-sessional students and assessments during the summer months whilst our regular Moodle site is being upgraded. The site also allows for support of pre-registered students who may not become full UoP students. These are students which can’t be supported on our regular Moodle site for security reasons. This upgrade is essential for the ongoing operation of the site owing to the age of the current version. A number of plugins must be decommissioned as part of the upgrade as these plugins no longer work in Moodle 4.5. Most of the plugins listed have not been used extensively on the site. Custom Certificate Course Merchant integration to purchase courses online Webex Accessibility block All-or-nothing question type (1000 questions used historically, the question type is no longer supported in Moodle 4.5) Adaptive multi-part question behaviour TinyMCE editor Panopto button The removal of these plugins is essential to ensure the site can be upgraded to Moodle 4.5. No significant issues are anticipated by removing the plugins. All-or-nothing questions will need to be reauthored using another question type in advance of any pre-sessional assessment activities in the future. pages.title: Showcase your AI Teaching Practice | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: AI is becoming an integral part of teaching and we know our colleagues are finding innovative and creative ways to use it in their classrooms. From boosting student engagement to designing assessments, the possibilities are endless! CADI is compiling examples of how AI is being used in learning and…
pages.content: AI is becoming an integral part of teaching and we know our colleagues are finding innovative and creative ways to use it in their classrooms. From boosting student engagement to designing assessments, the possibilities are endless! CADI is compiling examples of how AI is being used in learning and teaching, with the aim of sharing these insights through short, focused workshops led by staff who want to showcase their practice. If you’re already using AI in your teaching, this is your chance to: Share your innovative ideas with colleagues across UoP Inspire new approaches to teaching and learning Contribute to a growing community of AI practice Submit your example via the MS Form and help us highlight the amazing work happening across the University. Let’s celebrate creativity in teaching and show how AI can enhance the student experience. You can find more information on Leveraging AI in Learning and Teaching .

The CADI and IS teams recently completed a significant once in a decade activity to migrate our Moodle environment to a cloud environment supported by Catalyst IT Europe. pages.content: Our new Moodle environment is ready and available to staff and students. The CADI and IS teams recently completed a significant once in a decade activity to migrate our Moodle environment to a cloud environment supported by Catalyst IT Europe. This work was essential to provide much needed service resilience for the service moving forward. The migration of modules to our new environment for TB1 and year-long modules is complete. Please contact cadi-digital@port.ac.uk if you do not have access to your module. You’ll find a clarification below about where your module is hosted. 2025/26 Modules – moodle.port.ac.uk Our new cloud-based Moodle environment is now live at moodle.port.ac.uk . This environment holds new TB1 and year-long modules for 2025/26. 2024/25 Modules – moodlearchive.port.ac.uk Trailing and third attempt assessments should be conducted on moodlearchive.port.ac.uk which holds previous 2024/25 modules. We have a number of small issues which are being rectified after the migration. Panopto access A script was run on the 10th Sept to enable access for staff to their previous Panopto videos. Access to Panopto videos on Moodle should now be working for all users. H5P Resources As of 24th September all H5P resources within Moodle are now working as expected. The CADI team recently manually fixed around 900 H5P resources which couldn't be migrated automatically. Please contact cadi-digital@port.ac.uk if you have any further questions about H5P or other Moodle related que

This is your opportunity to showcase your exceptional contribution to learning and teaching on a national platform. The University of Portsmouth has a proud history of success in both the NTFS and CATE. This highlights the outstanding quality of our staff and their commitment to student success. We are now seeking nominations from individual staff and teams who exemplify excellence in teaching and learning. If you believe that you or your team have made significant contributions that have enhanced the student experience, please submit an expression of interest. pages.title: Alternative Summative Assessment Pilot  | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: We are delighted to share that the University’s Alternative Summative Assessment Pilot is being extended for a second year. The pilot explores the extent to which offering students a choice of summative assessment instruments can enhance inclusivity, improve the student experience and support learni…
pages.content: We are delighted to share that the University’s Alternative Summative Assessment Pilot is being extended for a second year. The pilot explores the extent to which offering students a choice of summative assessment instruments can enhance inclusivity, improve the student experience and support learning outcomes without compromising academic rigour or standards, or significantly adding to colleagues' workload. In 2024–25, around 20 modules across all Faculties took part with very positive student feedback. For the coming year, we aim to expand the number and range of modules involved. We would especially like to include a greater variety of alternative assessment instruments. Colleagues are reminded that this is a pilot exercise: modules must be formally signed up with CADI to participate. This ensures clear oversight, robust evaluation, compliance with PSRB and academic regulations, and support from CADI's Academic Innovation and Digital teams. Workshops for Module Coordinators will begin in September, and participating staff will be supported in designing and communicating assessment choices. To register your interest, please complete the MS Form and submit by Monday 29th September .

pages.content: Migration status of modules for 2025/26 The migration of our Moodle environment to a cloud supplier (Catalyst IT Europe) is progressing well. Over 75% of TB1 and year-long modules for 2025/26 have been successfully migrated to our new cloud-based Moodle environment. Once your module has been successfully migrated, a pink banner will appear at the top of your current Moodle module with a link to your new cloud-based module. More information about the migration batches can be found in our cloud move tracker article . Load Testing (11-14th August) Our cloud-based Moodle environment will provide a robust and scalable environment to handle huge user traffic at busy times such as the start of term and during assessment periods. The Catalyst team will be conducting load testing on the platform during 11-14th August to ensure the platform meets the needs of our students and staff. The platform will be unavailable to users at this time. Cut-Over Week (18-22nd August) At the start of Thursday, 21st August, the web address for our current on-campus Live Moodle Environment will be changed to moodlearchive.port.ac.uk . At this time, our new cloud-based Moodle environment will become moodle.port.ac.uk . It is anticipated that all live ongoing modules where students are still being actively taught will be migrated on Thursday 21st August. This may continue into Friday 22nd August, if the weight of modules is too great to process in one day. Both Moodle environments will be unavailable to st

pages.title: Reduced scope for Wiseflow in upcoming academic year | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: The decision has been made to limit Wiseflow’s rollout in 2025/26 to specific assessment scenarios. The following assessment scenarios are well supported with Wiseflow. The platform offers key functionality to support the effective delivery of these assessments. Double-blind marking of final-year pr…
pages.content: The decision has been made to limit Wiseflow’s rollout in 2025/26 to specific assessment scenarios. The following assessment scenarios are well supported with Wiseflow. The platform offers key functionality to support the effective delivery of these assessments. Double-blind marking of final-year projects Reflective portfolios Large file submissions (up to 5Gb) The reduction in scope for 2025/26 will allow development time for essential additional functionality in Wiseflow to support University of Portsmouth students and staff. An updated version of the Master Module Spreadsheet will be available after 5 September for staff to provide essential information about their summative assessments to ensure effective support from CADI and Academic Services staff. The spreadsheet will offer an Assessment Suggestion based on the type of assessment artefact students will be submitting. The logic behind this suggestion will be adjusted to reflect the reduced scope of the Wiseflow rollout in 2025/26. Academic staff will continue to be free to request a Turnitin or Moodle assignment if either tool fits their assessment scenario well. Staff will be able to create their own assessments and identify this intention on the MMS. Questions and Support If you have any questions about online assessment or Wiseflow, please contact cadi-digital@port.ac.uk .

pages.excerpt: Professor Anne Murphy Deputy Vice Chancellor (Educaton) Anne joined UoP in March 2021 as Executive Dean of Humanities and Social Sciences. She was appointed to the role of DVC Education in November 2023. She is responsible for the strategic leadership of all educational matters and oversight across…
pages.content: Professor Anne Murphy Deputy Vice Chancellor (Educaton) Anne joined UoP in March 2021 as Executive Dean of Humanities and Social Sciences. She was appointed to the role of DVC Education in November 2023. She is responsible for the strategic leadership of all educational matters and oversight across a wide range of University matters ensuring the provision of an excellent education and experience for students. Anne previously worked at the University of Hertfordshire where she was the Dean of the School of Humanities. Her first career was in the City trading interest rate and foreign exchange derivatives. Her research focuses on early modern financial markets and investment behaviour, and the organisation and management of the 18th-century Bank of England. Anne has published extensively on financial, social and economic history. She won the Economic History Society’s first monograph prize in 2010 for her monograph ‘The Origins of English Financial Markets: investment and speculation before the South Sea Bubble'. Her most recent book is 'Virtuous Bankers: a day in the life of the eighteenth-century Bank of England.' Visit Anne's Research Profile on Pure

pages.title: Meet Ale | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Professor Ale Armellini Dean of Education and Digital Innovation Prof Alejandro (Ale) Armellini leads digital learning, learning innovation and pedagogic transformation, including on-campus and online provision, across all Faculties at the University of Portsmouth. He developed the Digital Success P…
pages.content: Professor Ale Armellini Dean of Education and Digital Innovation Prof Alejandro (Ale) Armellini leads digital learning, learning innovation and pedagogic transformation, including on-campus and online provision, across all Faculties at the University of Portsmouth. He developed the Digital Success Plan for Learning and Teaching, which became the centrepiece of the University’s Education Strategy . Reporting to the DVC (Education), Ale is the strategic lead for the Centre for Academic and Digital Innovation (CADI). Before joining Portsmouth, Ale was Dean of Learning and Teaching at the University of Northampton, where he led the redesign of all programmes for active blended learning (ABL). His work shaped Northampton’s flagship Waterside campus, which opened in 2018 – without lecture theatres, among other radical features. He publishes widely and is a global reference point in areas including large-scale institutional transformation, ABL, learning design and innovative teaching practices across different modes of study. Ale is a National Teaching Fellow, a Principal Fellow of the Higher Education Academy and a Fellow of the Royal Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce in the UK. He holds visiting professorships at several universities and is active in external examining and consultancy globally. Visit Ale's Research Profile on Pure

pages.title: Meet Andy | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Dr Andy Clegg Head of Academic Innovation Dr Andy Clegg brings over 30 years of experience in Higher Education (HE) and industry to enhance student learning through currency, legitimacy, and credibility. As a Senior Fellow, National Teaching Fellow, CATE award holder, and Associate Professor in Acad…
pages.content: Dr Andy Clegg Head of Academic Innovation Dr Andy Clegg brings over 30 years of experience in Higher Education (HE) and industry to enhance student learning through currency, legitimacy, and credibility. As a Senior Fellow, National Teaching Fellow, CATE award holder, and Associate Professor in Academic Innovation and Enhancement, Andy has a sustained track record of leading strategic educational change and delivering real enhancement to learning and teaching. He specialises in tackling contemporary challenges and ‘wicked’ problems in HE through innovative curriculum design and creative pedagogic solutions. His academic interests span active blended learning, technology-enhanced learning, employability, student transition and communities, gamification, assessment and feedback, and education for sustainable development. A passionate advocate for creativity and play in HE, Andy is also a certified trainer in LEGO® Serious Play® (LSP). Andy helped design and now leads on enABLe ( enable.port.ac.uk ), the University of Portsmouth’s team-based approach to learning design that underpins its Blended and Connected model. He was instrumental in shaping the enABLe framework and designing the activities in its widely used toolkit. The framework has been recognised nationally by JISC and the Office for Students (OfS) as a sector-leading example of learning design practice. Andy is now spearheading the dissemination of enABLe across the UK and internationally. In addition to his work on l

pages.title: Meet Elaine | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Elaine Trise Administrator and Personal Assistant Elaine is the Personal Assistant to Professor Ale Armellini and Dr Andy Clegg and plays a key role in supporting the wider administrative functions of the CADI Team. She has been with the University for five years, bringing strong organisational and…
pages.content: Elaine Trise Administrator and Personal Assistant Elaine is the Personal Assistant to Professor Ale Armellini and Dr Andy Clegg and plays a key role in supporting the wider administrative functions of the CADI Team. She has been with the University for five years, bringing strong organisational and communication skills to the role following a career break to raise her children. Before joining the University, Elaine built a successful career in corporate marketing within international organisations. She holds a BA (Hons) in Business Studies from Bournemouth University and a Postgraduate Diploma in Strategic Marketing from the University of Portsmouth. Elaine brings a blend of professional marketing expertise and administrative efficiency to her work, contributing to the smooth operation of the team.

pages.title: Meet Sarah | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Sarah Blows Administrative Assistant Sarah has worked at the University for over twenty years and is a dedicated and detail-oriented administrative professional. She began working at the University’s Additional Support and Disability Advice Centre, supporting students and facilitating access to spec…
pages.content: Sarah Blows Administrative Assistant Sarah has worked at the University for over twenty years and is a dedicated and detail-oriented administrative professional. She began working at the University’s Additional Support and Disability Advice Centre, supporting students and facilitating access to specialist resources. In 2013, Sarah moved to DCQE, where she became responsible for managing internal module and course surveys, supporting and maintaining the EvaSys survey system, and serving as a key point of contact for survey-related enquiries. In the past, Sarah's role has expanded to include acting as Personal Assistant to the Director of DCQE, as well as supporting Academic Development. Now, as administrator for CADI, she manages APEX programme queries, assists with organising academic events and helps to ensure the smooth running of the department.

pages.title: Meet Matthew | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Dr Matthew Fleet Senior Lecturer in Higher Education Dr Matthew Fleet is a Senior Lecturer in Education with extensive experience in curriculum design, inclusive pedagogy, and international education. With a background in school and college leadership, and a Doctorate in Education from the Universit…
pages.content: Dr Matthew Fleet Senior Lecturer in Higher Education Dr Matthew Fleet is a Senior Lecturer in Education with extensive experience in curriculum design, inclusive pedagogy, and international education. With a background in school and college leadership, and a Doctorate in Education from the University of Winchester, Matthew brings a practitioner-informed perspective to curriculum enhancement and professional development. His work focuses on educational innovation, inclusive teaching, and curriculum development, with particular interests in closing the awarding gap, student engagement, sustainability, and supporting international learners. Matthew has delivered teacher training and consultancy across the UK and internationally, working with educators and institutions in the Middle East and Southeast Asia. Most recently, he has supported curriculum reform and professional development initiatives in Jordan and China. At Portsmouth, Matthew supports curriculum innovation and staff development through CADI, drawing on his expertise in assessment, inclusive practice, and pedagogical leadership. Areas of interest: Closing the awarding gap Sustainability Student Engagement International students Promoting inclusivity in higher education

pages.title: Meet Maria | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Maria Hutchinson Senior Lecturer in Learning Design Maria is a Senior Lecturer in Learning Design and leads on creative, team- and evidence-based approaches to Learning Design and pedagogic innovation. She supports teams in achieving teaching excellence from conceptualisation, design, teaching pract…
pages.content: Maria Hutchinson Senior Lecturer in Learning Design Maria is a Senior Lecturer in Learning Design and leads on creative, team- and evidence-based approaches to Learning Design and pedagogic innovation. She supports teams in achieving teaching excellence from conceptualisation, design, teaching practice and evaluation. Maria leads CADI’s Learning Design team to enable academic and services teams of staff across all disciplines to (re)design courses and modules through facilitating enABLe workshops (UoP's team-based approach to curriculum design). She also leads wider enABLe activities, including capacity building initiatives in Learning Design, such as training and development of new enABLe facilitators. As a Senior Fellow of the HEA, Maria supports colleagues’ personal and professional development through mentoring and review activities on the APEX programme for both written and professional conversation routes to Fellowship. She also supports staff development by leading on the design, development, approval and delivery of credit-bearing modules such as “Teach Well: Principles to Practice”. Maria is a Senior Fellow of the HEA, has a MSc in Applied AI and Data Science, a PgCert in Learning and facilitates Lego Serious Play. Areas of interest: enABLe team-based curriculum design workshops Learning design and pedagogic guidance on best practice Transforming the Experience of Students Through Assessment (TESTA) Alternative assessments and innovation is assessments Course Leader/

pages.title: Meet Mary | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Mary Watkins Senior Lecturer in Higher Education Mary is a Senior Lecturer in Higher Education within the Centre for Academic Development and Innovation. She joined the University in 2005 on a fixed-term maternity cover contract with the Careers & Employability Service. She later transitioned to the…
pages.content: Mary Watkins Senior Lecturer in Higher Education Mary is a Senior Lecturer in Higher Education within the Centre for Academic Development and Innovation. She joined the University in 2005 on a fixed-term maternity cover contract with the Careers & Employability Service. She later transitioned to the Student Finance Centre as a Student Finance Officer where she enjoyed facilitating financial support workshops and helping students secure additional funding. In 2008 Mary became one of the first Online Course Developers in the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences designing online courses for government employees. Over the years she expanded her knowledge of educational technology and active blended learning. In 2021 Mary moved into a Learning Designer role where she had the opportunity to enhance students' learning journey by collaborating with course teams. Mary’s academic background includes postgraduate studies at the University of Portsmouth and the Open University where she gained firsthand experience as a part-time and distance learner. She completed the Teach Well module in 2024 and is a Senior Fellow of the Higher Education Academy (HEA). Mary's research interests focus on learning design frameworks and inclusive assessment strategies.

pages.title: Meet Sally | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Sally Cheng Learning Designer Sally has been part of the University of Portsmouth since 2001, working for many years as an Online Course Developer and Digital Learning Developer before moving into her current role as a Learning Designer in the CADI team. Throughout this time, she has supported staff…
pages.content: Sally Cheng Learning Designer Sally has been part of the University of Portsmouth since 2001, working for many years as an Online Course Developer and Digital Learning Developer before moving into her current role as a Learning Designer in the CADI team. Throughout this time, she has supported staff across a wide range of disciplines with designing accessible, engaging, and effective digital learning experiences. Her practice is grounded in a strong belief in the power of play, creativity, and curiosity in higher education. She has a particular interest in play-based learning and gamification, and enjoys helping educators design activities that spark intrinsic motivation and support students to explore their disciplines in fun and engaging ways. Sally is also passionate about building inclusive learning communities and fostering a sense of belonging. She brings this focus to her work by encouraging approaches that are student-centred, purposeful, and carefully designed.

pages.title: Meet Joanna | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Joanna Clarke Learning Designer Joanna has over 20 years of experience in education, having worked as both a lecturer in Further Education (FE) and a teacher trainer. Her teaching background includes English as a Foreign Language (EFL) and English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL). She holds a…
pages.content: Joanna Clarke Learning Designer Joanna has over 20 years of experience in education, having worked as both a lecturer in Further Education (FE) and a teacher trainer. Her teaching background includes English as a Foreign Language (EFL) and English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL). She holds a PGCE in Post-Compulsory Education and an MA in Applied Linguistics and TESOL, which have equipped her with a strong foundation in pedagogy and language education. Alongside her teaching roles, Joanna held various positions focused on integrating learning technologies into the classroom. This included developing expertise in using Moodle and Audience Response Systems, providing training and support to colleagues in these areas. In 2022, she joined the University of Portsmouth’s eLearn team and then transitioned into her current role as a Learning Designer with the CADI team. In this role, she remains committed to enhancing student engagement by incorporating active learning strategies, gamification, and authentic content, while ensuring accessibility for all learners. Joanna is passionate about bringing these values into her work, continually striving to create inclusive and engaging learning experiences. Areas of interest: Course and Module redesign Gamification in education Audience Response Systems (particularly Padlet, Vevox and Nearpod) Student Engagement International students Generative AI as a support tool

pages.title: Meet Jenny | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Jenny Cox Learning Designer After graduating with a degree in Architectural Technology and working in the construction industry for 12 years, Jenny retrained as a teacher of mathematics in further education and earned her PGCE in 2018. She initially taught GCSE maths resit students at Chichester Col…
pages.content: Jenny Cox Learning Designer After graduating with a degree in Architectural Technology and working in the construction industry for 12 years, Jenny retrained as a teacher of mathematics in further education and earned her PGCE in 2018. She initially taught GCSE maths resit students at Chichester College before coming to work at the University of Portsmouth as a Learning Support Tutor in Civil Engineering and Surveying. Jenny is interested in data analytics and was involved in a project investigating the ethnicity awarding gap at the University. She went on to complete an MSc in Statistics and is interested in finding ways to better utilise quantitative data to inform teaching practice. Jenny began working as a Learning Designer at Portsmouth on a part-time basis in 2022 with a particular focus on supporting students through the transition from FE to HE and strategies for closing the awarding gap. Alongside her Learning Designer role, Jenny is currently studying for a PhD in Sustainable AI with the University of Southampton.

pages.title: Meet Richard C | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Richard Crabtree Learning Designer Richard joined the University of Portsmouth in 2017, bringing with him over a decade of teaching and lecturing experience in computing, business, and ICT across the UK and internationally. He began work at the University as a Senior Online Course Developer before b…
pages.content: Richard Crabtree Learning Designer Richard joined the University of Portsmouth in 2017, bringing with him over a decade of teaching and lecturing experience in computing, business, and ICT across the UK and internationally. He began work at the University as a Senior Online Course Developer before becoming its first Learning Designer in 2021. With a BSc in Computer Animation and a PGCE with QTS, Richard combines a strong foundation in subject knowledge and pedagogy. His MSc in Video Game Technology brings a creative, interactive perspective to learning design, while his MBA in Strategic Leadership supports his ability to align academic goals with institutional strategy. This diverse academic background allows Richard to bridge the gap between technical innovation, effective teaching practice, and strategic thinking. Working closely with academic colleagues, students, and external stakeholders, Richard supports the design and development of learning at every level—from overall course structure to session activities and assessments. He is particularly interested in active blended learning, not only as a method to improve engagement but also as a pathway to more meaningful, outcome-driven educational experiences. His design approach is grounded in the principles of constructive alignment, ensuring coherence between learning outcomes, activities, and assessment. Areas of interest: Course and module redesign Assessment redesign AI in assessment Online learning Student engagement

pages.title: Meet Becky | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Becky Holman Learning Designer Becky has worked for the University of Portsmouth since 2007 and was most recently a Learning Technologist for Degree Apprenticeships before moving into her current role of Learning Designer for CADI. With 7 years of expertise in designing, developing, and implementing…
pages.content: Becky Holman Learning Designer Becky has worked for the University of Portsmouth since 2007 and was most recently a Learning Technologist for Degree Apprenticeships before moving into her current role of Learning Designer for CADI. With 7 years of expertise in designing, developing, and implementing innovative digital learning resources for degree apprenticeships, Becky has vast experience of collaborating with academic staff and external stakeholders to create interactive and accessible online learning environments through the constructive alignment of learning objectives, activities, and assessments. Her particular interests are to facilitate ways that academics can create engaging, yet meaningful content and sparking their creativity using narrative arcs that can flow through an activity, module or course. Her personal values mean that she is passionate about using technology to create meaningful and engaging content, whilst supporting diverse learner needs. Becky takes CPD very seriously and is always looking to inform her practice and learn new things. She is an Associate Fellow of the HEA and has undertaken the ‘Teach Well: Principles of Practice' Level 7 module, which focuses on learning and design approaches, and what it means to teach well across different modes of study. pages.title: Meet Jamie | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Jamie Meekins Learning Designer Jamie is an experienced and passionate educationalist who previously taught in FE and secondary education. With a PGCE in Post Compulsory Education, DTTLS and also has a wealth of knowledge and expertise in Media Production. He has worked across the University first i…
pages.content: Jamie Meekins Learning Designer Jamie is an experienced and passionate educationalist who previously taught in FE and secondary education. With a PGCE in Post Compulsory Education, DTTLS and also has a wealth of knowledge and expertise in Media Production. He has worked across the University first in the faculty of Business and Law as a Learning Technologist contributing his knowledge of creating successful interactive/video learning artefacts and innovative module/Moodle design. Since 2022, Jamie has been working as a part of the London campus project, starting out as a Learning Technologist and progressing to a Learning Designer. He has made a significant impact within the London Campus by taking the learning and teaching approach through from ideation to an operational teaching institution. Namely around the purposeful adoption of block teaching using enABLe and supporting effective assessment redesign. He has also implemented the use of new learning systems like MS365 and Wiseflow which have become an integral part of the teaching that happens at the London Campus. Jamie has an invested interest in the constant improvement of the student experience. pages.content: Chris Wood Learning Designer Chris joined Portsmouth University in August 2022 after teaching Music Technology and Media Studies at Portsmouth College for over 12 years. He led the College’s 1:1 “Creative and curious” iPad project, resulting in Portsmouth College becoming one of only two colleges in the UK to be recognised as an Apple Distinguished School. In 2019, he became an Apple Distinguished Educator – a recognition of how he continues to use Apple technology to transform teaching and learning, which has taken him all over the world. Chris is a published author and presents at conferences all over the world on teaching innovation. Chris thrives on helping academics redesign and develop their courses, contributing to the development of assessment practices that empower students to succeed in their academic pursuits by offering differentiated paths to the same learning outcome. He is committed to enhancing students' learning experience through technology and pedagogical innovation. When not working, Chris can be found playing guitar (which he has done for over 24 years now!); taking his whippet “Twiggy” for a walk; playing snooker or getting up at the crack of dawn to chase sunrises. Chris joins us as a Learning Designer and brings a wealth of experience to this role including staff training, pedagogical innovation, project management, driving technology-enhanced learning and creative thinking.

pages.title: Teaching Block 2 Assessments - Master Module Spreadsheet Information | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Based on feedback from academic teams and in consultation with senior Service Centre colleagues we have made improvements to the Master Module Spreadsheets for each faculty . Module coordinators must add information about their assessments by January 17th to ensure summative assessments are created…
pages.content: Based on feedback from academic teams and in consultation with senior Service Centre colleagues we have made improvements to the Master Module Spreadsheets for each faculty . Module coordinators must add information about their assessments by January 17th to ensure summative assessments are created in a timely manner for teaching block 2 . The Master Module Spreadsheet (MMS) must be completed to provide Academic Services and CADI with the necessary details of assessments being delivered throughout the academic year. We rely on these details being complete and accurate to enable critical support activities to be carried out. This includes processes such as Wiseflow/Moodle assignment and Dropbox creation, identification of In Class Tests and Exams, Extenuating Circumstances processes and effective resource planning. To assist in creating TB2 assessments, Moodle assignments will be re-enabled for academic colleagues alongside Wiseflow and Turnitin assignments. In most instances your assessment cannot be created until this information is added to the MMS . Can I set up my own summative assessments? pages.content: What is WISEflow? WISEflow is a cloud-based end-to-end digital assessment platform that supports the assessment and feedback lifecycle for students, assessors, administrators and assessment authors. WISEflow is a role-based system which enables activities to be allocated to specific users based on the part that they play in the assessment process. This website provides user guides and help articles for creating WISEflow assessments. It is primarily aimed at supporting academics and support staff at the University of Portsmouth, but also includes some user guides that can be shared with students. Our team is working on improving the site and providing guidance on how WISEflow can enhance assessment design practices. WISEflow Overview View transcript How do I access WISEflow? pages.excerpt: In 2024, the University of Portsmouth moved to the use of a categorical marking scale for assessing student work. What is Categorical Marking? Categorical Marking is a method of assessing work, which associates each classification with a percentage score, and a description. Rather than using the ful…
pages.content: In 2024, the University of Portsmouth moved to the use of a categorical marking scale for assessing student work. What is Categorical Marking? Categorical Marking is a method of assessing work, which associates each classification with a percentage score, and a description. Rather than using the full 0-100 percentage points, in most cases, each classification has a high, mid and low point, ending in 2, 5 and 8. For example, a 2:1 is as follows: 68% = 2.1 (upper) 65% = 2.1 (middle) 62% = 2.1 (lower) Work that is typically characterised by evidence of the following: A good or very good level of knowledge and understanding of key concepts and principles Good or very good awareness of subject and/or practice Able to describe and use a range of major concepts, theories and methodologies in a good or very good level of detail Strong arguments which offer good analysis of key issues Use of a broad/wide range of sources and/or evidence to support work May contain some minor errors and/or minor areas for improvement Clearly structured, robust and persuasive arguments, lucidly written, uses clear and accurate expression You can view the full Categorical Marking scale for both undergraduate courses and postgraduate courses under "Supporting documentation" on the Academic Registry website . Note: the Categorical Marking scale is applied at the assignment level, so the overall mark for a module with more than one assignment could still allow scores outside these 2,5,8. pages.excerpt: What are Alternative Assessments? Alternative Assessments encompass diverse, non-traditional evaluation methods aimed at assessing a student's ability to apply their knowledge, rather than simply recalling information they've learned. Examples of Alternative Assessments are: Portfolios: A curated co…
pages.content: What are Alternative Assessments? Alternative Assessments encompass diverse, non-traditional evaluation methods aimed at assessing a student's ability to apply their knowledge, rather than simply recalling information they've learned. Examples of Alternative Assessments are: Portfolios: A curated compilation of student work demonstrating their learning progression over time. Projects: Extended tasks that require students to apply knowledge and skills in practical, real-world scenarios. Open-ended questions: Prompts that encourage students to offer detailed and thoughtful responses beyond a simple answer. Oral presentations: Opportunities for students to share their work or ideas with a small audience. Self-assessment: A process where students evaluate the quality of their own work. Written compositions: Assignments in which students express their thoughts or knowledge on a given topic through writing. How are Alternative Assessments being used at the University of Portsmouth? Jovana Radulovic and Prof Ale Armellini are currently leading on a pilot at UoP for alternative assessments where some students on courses across all Faculties are being given a choice of summative assessment instruments. The aim of the pilot is to enable students to demonstrate achievement of learning outcomes in a variety of ways, outside of traditional assessment norms and practices. pages.content: Wiseflow provides a mature workflow that enables External Examiners to access student submissions within the platform. This can save significant time compared to downloading and transferring student submissions. Module coordinators can select which assessments External Examiners can view by allocating External Examiners to specific students' work . This approach allows for access to be given easily to all submissions or a subset of student work that spans the full range of marks . Manager access is needed within Wiseflow to allocate student work to External Examiners. Module Coordinators, Academic Services and CADI Digital Learning Developers have Manager access to Wiseflow assessments by default. You can request support allocating student submissions to External Examiners via this Service Desk form the CADI Digital team will need to know whether all student submissions should be allocated to the External Examiner or a specific sample. Importantly, these approaches comply with the University's External Examiner Policy . Specifically, 3.2.3 outlines the responsibility of External Examiners in terms of being provided with a sample of no less than 20% of the total assessment for the module. 3.3.1 Outlines the External Examiners' rights to see any work submitted for assessment for any module for which they are responsible for. pages.content: All assessed work is subject to verification or double-blind marking in line with the University’s Assessment regulations . This article covers the process for verification and double-blind marking within Wiseflow, with user guides available to support the process. Wiseflow offers mature functionality to support the verification of assessments (Reviewer approval) and double-blind marking of major project modules (Marker Agreement). Recent improvements to mark verification in Wiseflow Based on feedback from academics in teaching block one, 2024/25 standard assessments, which are subject to marks verification in Wiseflow, are now set up using the ‘Reviewer approval’ process, which is much more closely aligned with existing University practice and our assessment regulations. This configuration makes adjusting marks by someone other than the original marker far easier. In most cases, it is most appropriate for the Module Coordinator to amend marks after the marks verification process has been completed, although this permission is available to all module lecturers in the event the MC is unavailable. Marks Verification & Mark Finalisation in Wiseflow (Single Marker) By volume, roughly 85% of the University’s assessments involve a marks verification process. pages.content: Managing money is a major concern for students, with rising living costs, increased reliance on part-time work and limited financial support affecting wellbeing and university life. The following resources provide insights and guidance: NatWest: Student Living Index 2024 This annual survey offers an overview of what students earn, how they spend their money and what they budget for. It highlights the increasing reliance on parental support and part-time employment, as well as the rising costs of living. This survey provides accessible and impactful statistics that help to understand the worsening financial situation of students in the UK. OfS: Studying during rises in the cost of living The OfS surveyed over 4000 students to understand the impact that rising living costs are having on students and their decision to continue studying. It provides a list of recommended actions by universities to improve financial assistance. The report covers issues such as living costs, the increase in part-time working, meeting food needs and missing out on the full student experience. University of Edinburgh Podcast: Student Wellbeing and the Hidden Costs of Financial Hardship To build on the statistics in the above reports, this podcast discusses the impact of financial hardship on students. The podcast is led by Tessa Warinner, Student Wellbeing Adviser at the University of Edinburgh and chats with Grace Clark, president of the University of Edinburgh branch of the 93% club. This is a foundation for quality teaching in general; our teaching is only fit for purpose if it works for the diverse range of students at our institutions and treats them all like they matter. Rather than an end goal, inclusion is a mindset which promotes critical thinking and cultural transformation. pages.content: Mental health significantly affects university students’ experiences, impacting academic performance, engagement, social life and retention. In recent years, mental health in UK higher education has become a priority concern, with research showing that students face increasing levels of stress, anxiety and depression. Websites HEPI: Explaining the rise in student mental health and wellbeing issues HE Professional: How Does Poor Mental Health Affect Students? 6 Key Impacts HEPI: University Mental Health Day: Why we encourage students to disclose their mental health conditions and why you should too HE Professional: 10 Ways to Combat Student Loneliness: A Guide for Higher Education Professionals Universities UK: Stepchange: Mentally Healthy Universities UoP: This is just the beginning – male students’ mental health Following Movember in 2024, UoP raised the issue of men’s mental health in the article above. On this page, you can hear from some of our male students about their struggles with mental health and how they are working through it. Male students are also much less likley to seek support for their wellbeing; the proportion of male UG students at UoP who have contacted wellbeing for a consultation has remained at 9% in each of the last three years. In addition, the proportion of female students with a mental health condition has more than doubled over seven years.

pages.content: Capture your message clearly and professionally with a "talking head" video. This is when the camera’s focus of the shot is entirely on the presenter’s head and shoulders as they convey their perspective, insight, and context to a specific audience. To create a more dynamic and engaging experience, supplementary elements, such as cutaways (B-roll footage), can be included. These elements enhance and complement the main message, helping to create a richer narrative and keeping the audience’s attention. We recommend these shots are scripted for a clear and polished presentation. Our studio camera is equipped with a teleprompter to help you stay on message while maintaining eye contact with the camera. Alternatively, if you prefer a more natural and conversational approach for a more authentic expression of your ideas, we can record your message without a script. CADI in Action Many of the team involved with these productions are not just simply taking simple talking head videos. The staff involved are in charge of supporting the recording in the studio, setting up the lighting and picture assembly in post-production. These are then brought to life with bespoke graphics, captions, music bed, title cards, animations, b-roll footage and cutaways. Light's, camera, learning! See how CADI helped bring these Piece to Camera examples to life. Assessment Reasonable Adjustments This is an Assessment Reasonable Adjustments video by Dr Charles Barker, the Head of School. pages.content: Learning Glass is a state-of-the-art media recording setup where instructors can write, annotate and illustrate concepts directly on an illuminated transparent Learning Glass while being recorded by a camera facing the screen. This approach is particularly beneficial for our learners, as it merges traditional whiteboard techniques with modern video technology. Perfect for lectures, tutorials, and explainer videos, the Learning Glass provides a dynamic and engaging method for conveying complex ideas, helping students connect with the material more effectively. Our dedicated team of experts are available to collaborate with you in planning all the technical aspects of filming to ensure the result meets the brief. We are committed to making your project a success, ensuring it is immersive, resonates with your students and effectively conveys your message. The CADI have produced over 300 Learning Glass videos and this medium has proved to be very impactful and engaging for students. CADI in Action See how the CADI team has supported the production of impactful Learning Glass content across campus. Simplifying Fractions This excerpt features a Learning Glass video by Ms. Kirsten Williams from the Faculty of Technology. It highlights her innovative approach to sharing detailed exam revision materials, using the Learning Glass to effectively address student knowledge gaps and prepare them for their final assessments. pages.excerpt: This involves using our cutting-edge 360-degree camera to capture high-quality, fully interactive video that enhances educational content, making it not only visually appealing but also deeply engaging. With this approach, learners can interact with diverse elements within the content—such as clicka…
pages.content: This involves using our cutting-edge 360-degree camera to capture high-quality, fully interactive video that enhances educational content, making it not only visually appealing but also deeply engaging. With this approach, learners can interact with diverse elements within the content—such as clickable features that provide additional information or real-time demonstrations—to encourage deeper exploration and understanding of the material. This method not only makes learning more visually appealing but also fosters an enjoyable and compelling educational experience, promoting active involvement and a strong sense of presence. pages.excerpt: We have compiled essential tips to help ensure you have a successful recording session with our team. pages.content: We have compiled essential tips to help ensure you have a successful recording session with our team. Please take a moment to review the following suggestions as you prepare for your session. If you have any questions, do not hesitate to contact the CADI Media Production team at cadi-digital@port.ac.uk . Preparation Practice sounding natural. Have water available to clear your throat. Ensure your mobile device is silenced. Appearance Avoid clothing with colours that blend in with the background (Avoid green) Avoid shiny, patterned, pin-striped or dotted fabrics. Avoid noisy accessories that could interfere with your audio. Delivery Look at the camera/teleprompter at all times Speak naturally and more slowly than you typically would in person. Keep your hand movements/gestures to a minimum. Stand/sit up straight and keep your shoulders relaxed. Answering Questions When responding to question prompts, begin by rephrasing the question at the start of your answer instead of diving directly into the response. For example, rather than stating, “Yes, in the main auditorium,” say, “The meeting will take place in the main auditorium.” Refrain from speaking while others are talking or responding; it helps maintain a clean and clear audio recording. Begin and conclude your responses with a few seconds of silence while looking directly into the camera. Start and end your answers clearly (Avoid starting every sentence with “so" and "um" and refrain from upward intonations at the end, like

pages.title: Digifest 2026: From disruption to direction | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Colleagues from the Centre for Academic and Digital Innovation attended Digifest 2026 , joining a national community of educators and innovators exploring the evolving role of digital technology in higher education (HE) and further education (FE). pages.content: Colleagues from the Centre for Academic and Digital Innovation attended Digifest 2026 , joining a national community of educators and innovators exploring the evolving role of digital technology in higher education (HE) and further education (FE). This year’s event focused on moving “from disruption to direction”, with a strong emphasis on how institutions can respond to rapid technological change in ways that are ethical, inclusive, and human-centred. A key theme throughout was the growing impact of generative AI on teaching, learning, and assessment. Sessions encouraged us not only to explore what AI can do, but to critically reflect on how we use it, and whether we are asking the right questions as educators. Professor Danny Liu’s session particularly highlighted the importance of designing learning experiences that prioritise critical thinking and student agency in an AI-enabled world. The keynote from Melati Wijsen offered a different but equally powerful perspective, focusing on youth leadership and the importance of taking action to create meaningful change. This provided a valuable reminder that innovation is not just about technology, but about people, purpose, and impact. Beyond the sessions, Digifest also provided an opportunity to explore a wide range of emerging digital tools and platforms. The exhibition space highlighted the pace of development across the sector and reinforced the importance of evaluating tools not just for functionality, but for their pedagogi

pages.title: Review of AI training | University of Portsmouth
pages.excerpt: Since March 2025, CADI has facilitated 30 workshops from our initial three generative AI sessions — AI in the Classroom: Foundations and First Steps , Harnessing AI: Enhance Teaching, Empower Learning , and AI and Assessment – Academic Integrity and Innovation — attended by almost 300 colleagues. We…
pages.content: Since March 2025, CADI has facilitated 30 workshops from our initial three generative AI sessions — AI in the Classroom: Foundations and First Steps , Harnessing AI: Enhance Teaching, Empower Learning , and AI and Assessment – Academic Integrity and Innovation — attended by almost 300 colleagues. We have also run 20 bespoke workshops, supporting nearly 500 colleagues to build confidence and make meaningful use of AI in their teaching. Colleagues have reported clear, practical impact. One attendee noted that a group activity “ produced a great suggestion for a workshop I’m running,” while another now plans to “bring AI into seminar sessions as an icebreaker and into assessments .” Others shared that they “ no longer feel overwhelmed ” and can now “ use AI tools more efficiently in preparation and teaching .” Many have begun “ adapting assessments to be more resilient to AI, ” “ using prompts to explore the limitations of LLMs ” and feeling more confident discussing academic integrity and AI with students. Our programme continues to evolve, with two new sessions in development, including From Outputs to Insight: Building Ethical and Critical AI Practice , launching in early 2026. We also continue to invite colleagues to share examples of AI in learning and teaching to build our institutional repository of practice.

pages.excerpt: Estranged Students and Care Leavers, whilst not having all the same experiences, can face similar difficulties in university. pages.content: Estranged Students and Care Leavers, whilst not having all the same experiences, can face similar difficulties in university. The term 'estranged' refers to a person who is no longer in contact with or is alienated from their family both physically and emotionally, while a care leaver refers to someone who has been in the care of a local authority for at least 13 weeks since their 14th birthday and who also has their 16th birthday while in care. In both cases, the lack of a support network can contribute to a lack of financial stability causing an estranged person or care leaver to have trouble finding stable accommodation and could cause them to struggle to keep up with workload from education providers while working enough hours to support themselves. This in turn may affect them in the forms of low attendance, low quality assessment submissions or feeling generally overwhelmed by the workload. All of these issues combined can make estranged people and care leavers extremely vulnerable to mental health conditions and leave them feeling isolated, especially during major annual holidays. For people in these positions, they may feel like university is not an option either due to unstable finances, lack of time or general imbalance in life. Here at the University of Portsmouth, it is our mission to ensure that every student has an equal opportunity to challenge themselves and pursue any aspirations they have.
